w: Wilde lounges on a quartz boulder in clothes carved from semi-precious stone — jade jacket, pink thulite collar, granite trousers — gazing across Merrion Square at his childhood home. Danny Osborne's 1997 figure wears a two-sided face: smiling one way, sombre the other. Dubliners call him 'The Fag on the Crag.'

The last of medieval Dublin's gates. The walled city once bristled with as many as nineteen of them, toll points taxing everything that entered; this 13th-century arch, punched through the older Hiberno-Norse wall behind St Audoen's Church, alone survives. Restored in 1976, still the pedestrian way up to High Street.

Thin Lizzy's frontman cast in bronze, bass in hand and mid stage-pose, outside Bruxelles on Harry Street where he used to drink. Paul Daly sculpted him, Leo Higgins cast him in the Liberties, and his mother Philomena unveiled it in August 2005 after years of campaigning. The boy, back in town for good.
